checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly translates the slide-based layout of the source PDF into a linear, accessible document. Multi-column elements, such as the grid of boxes on page 28 ('Future Initiatives') and the side-by-side comparisons (e.g., page 12, page 23, and page 26), are appropriately rendered to maintain the visual flow and intended meaning. The visual organization of information matches the source's intent.

Tables used in the source PDF to illustrate old website functionality (pages 16 and 18) are rendered as valid HTML tables. The headers are distinct, and rows/columns are clearly aligned, making the data easily readable for a sighted user. Form layouts shown as screenshots in the PDF (pages 22 and 23) are also rendered as structured tables that accurately reflect the source content.

All meaningful images and graphics from the source PDF are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. This includes the department seal on page 1, abstract technology backgrounds (pages 4, 27), icons (pages 5, 11, 29), and various screenshots used for comparison and demonstration throughout the presentation (e.g., mobile phone evolution on page 8, accessibility tool on page 13, and side-by-side website comparisons).

There is no evidence of duplicated body content. While headings appear in both the Table of Contents and the document body, this is expected accessible design. No paragraphs, tables, or image blocks are repeated within the body content itself.
